behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Rename detection is at the boundary of real git output semantics (e.g., rename status codes and similarity thresholds) plus CLI parsing. A pure in-process mock can easily miss real subprocess/output-format failures and double-count bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repo, commit a file, rename it (`git mv`), then run the CLI as a subprocess against that repo. Assert report shows 1 renamed, 0 added, 0 deleted for that change set.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary handling failures often occur from real git subprocess output, encoding/decoding, and OS-level file handling. Mocking diff output can hide actual crashes on binary blobs.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp repo, commit then modify/add a real binary file (e.g., random bytes), run the CLI subprocess, and assert exit code is success and binary changes are counted in the structured stdout report.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The no-commit case depends on actual git repo state and subprocess error modes (e.g., unresolved HEAD). A mock may not reproduce the exact failure boundary that occurs in real execution.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a brand-new git repo with zero commits, run the CLI subprocess, and assert successful exit plus an empty structured report on stdout (not an exception/error message).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on end-to-end interaction with real git history traversal and status interpretation across commits. In-process mocks can hide mismatches between expected and actual git behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Build a temp repo history with known numbers of added/deleted/renamed/modified files across multiple commits, run the CLI subprocess, and assert each reported category count matches the constructed ground truth.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Staged vs unstaged behavior relies on real git index/worktree semantics, which are external and subtle. Mocking typically hides discrepancies in how CLI commands query index vs working tree.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp repo, make the same file edits twice: once left unstaged and once staged (`git add`). Run the CLI subprocess for each state and assert identical structured output for equivalent changes.
